text: The surgeon attempts to create a preperitoneal flap, with the medial and lateral leaflets of the peritoneum converging at the inferior epigastric vessels.
The surgeon uses electrocautery to buzz and push the fat laterally, working on the lateral aspect of the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies crossing vessels near the inferior epigastric vessels. The antisac is visible, and the surgeon uses the cautery to gently push it away.
The surgeon carefully manipulates the antisac, gently pushing and sweeping it away to avoid disturbing the iliohypogastric nerve, which is located medial and above the muscle layer.
The surgeon approaches the pubic tubercle and Cooper's ligament, carefully maneuvering near the left iliac vein. The surgical field reveals minimal extra fat in the patient.
The surgeon maintains close proximity to the pubic bone during the procedure, ensuring precise manipulation of the surrounding tissue.
The surgeon follows the anatomical guidelines to extend beyond the symphysis pubis on the contralateral side, using an oblique angle to pull the hernia sac downward.
A small tear is visible in the peritoneal flap, which will typically be repaired during the closure phase of the procedure.
The vas deferens becomes visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon carefully maneuvers around the corner near the iliac vein, ensuring the mesh does not buckle as it is placed just below where it begins to fall underneath the vein.
The surgeon inspects the surgical field for a cord lipoma, maintaining proximity to the pubic bone while adhering to established procedural guidelines.
The surgeon positions the mesh, navigating medially to the coronamortis vein and between the coronamortis vein and the iliac vein, ensuring adequate space for the mesh placement.
The surgeon performs triangular fixation on the direct hernia, followed by high medial and high lateral fixation, ensuring secure placement of the mesh.
The surgeon closes the flap, aligning the medial umbilical ligament and the preperitoneal fat plane in a standard fashion.
